(also known as a **horizontally opposed-four engine** or **boxer engine**[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is a four-cylinder [piston engine](/wiki/Piston_engine "Piston engine") with two banks of cylinders lying on opposite sides of a common crankshaft. The most common type of flat-four engine is the **boxer-four engine**, each pair of opposed pistons moves inwards and outwards at the same time.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Horizontally opposed four-cylinder piston engine

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(December 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/UL350iS_ULPower_aircraft_engine.jpg/250px-UL350iS_ULPower_aircraft_engine.jpg)](/wiki/File:UL350iS_ULPower_aircraft_engine.jpg)

ULPower UL360iS aircraft piston engine

A **flat-four engine** (also known as a **horizontally opposed-four engine** or **boxer engine**[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is a four-cylinder [piston engine](/wiki/Piston_engine "Piston engine") with two banks of cylinders lying on opposite sides of a common crankshaft. The most common type of flat-four engine is the **boxer-four engine**, each pair of opposed pistons moves inwards and outwards at the same time.

A boxer-four engine has perfect primary and secondary balance, however, the two cylinder heads means the design is more expensive to produce than a [straight-four engine](/wiki/Straight-four_engine "Straight-four engine"). There is a minor, secondary unbalanced rotational torque pulse in the plane of the pistons, when a piston pair at one end of the engine is at TDC and the other pair at BDC. The TDC pair creates a torque greater than the BDC pair, so the net unbalanced torque pulse is the difference. The difference in TDC vs BDC inertial forces is explained in the [Engine balance](#Engine_balance) section. Boxer-four engines have been used in cars since 1897, especially by Volkswagen and Subaru. They have also occasionally been used in motorcycles and frequently in aircraft. [Cessna](/wiki/Cessna "Cessna") and [Piper](/wiki/Piper_Aircraft "Piper Aircraft") use flat four engines from [Lycoming](/wiki/Lycoming_Engines "Lycoming Engines") and [Continental](/wiki/Continental_Aerospace_Technologies "Continental Aerospace Technologies") in the most [common civil aircraft](/wiki/List_of_most-produced_aircraft "List of most-produced aircraft") in the world - the [Cessna 172](/wiki/Cessna_172 "Cessna 172"), and [Piper Cherokee](/wiki/Piper_Cherokee "Piper Cherokee"), while many [ultralight](/wiki/Ultralight_aviation "Ultralight aviation") and [LSA](/wiki/Light-sport_aircraft "Light-sport aircraft") planes use versions of the [Rotax 912](/wiki/Rotax_912 "Rotax 912").

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Design")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Vierzylinder_Boxermotor.gif/250px-Vierzylinder_Boxermotor.gif)](/wiki/File:Vierzylinder_Boxermotor.gif)

Boxer-four animation

In a flat-four engine, each pair of opposing [pistons](/wiki/Piston "Piston") successively moves inwards together and outwards together. The advantages of the boxer-four layout are perfect secondary vibration (resulting in minimal vibration), a low [centre of gravity](/wiki/Centre_of_gravity "Centre of gravity"), and a short engine length. Flat-four engine have successfully used [air cooling](/wiki/Air_cooling "Air cooling"), although air-cooled engines are noisier and have a lower power output than an equivalent engine with [liquid cooling](/wiki/Liquid_cooling "Liquid cooling"). In light aircraft, where lightness is of primary importance, air-cooling has traditionally been common.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The downsides of boxer-four engines (compared with inline-four engines) are their extra width, the increased costs associated with having two cylinder heads instead of one, and the long exhaust manifold required to achieve evenly spaced exhaust pulses.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Due to these factors, [straight-four engines](/wiki/Straight-four_engine "Straight-four engine") are more common in cars than are flat-four engines, and [V6 engines](/wiki/V6_engine "V6 engine") are often used where larger displacements are required.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Engine balance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Engine balance")]

The equal and opposing forces generated in a boxer-four engine result in perfect [secondary balance](/wiki/Secondary_balance "Secondary balance") (unlike the unbalanced vertical forces produced by inline-four engines). Boxer-four engines are therefore better suited to displacements above 2.0 L (122 cu in), since they do not require [balance shafts](/wiki/Balance_shaft "Balance shaft") to reduce the secondary vibration.

In a boxer engine, each cylinder is slightly offset from its opposing pair due to the distance between the [crankpin](/wiki/Crankpin "Crankpin") journals. This offset gives rise to a slight [rocking couple](/wiki/Couple_(mechanics) "Couple (mechanics)"),[[5]](#cite_note-Wilson-5): 27 but any resulting vibration is normally insufficient to require balance shafts.

As with all four-stroke engines of four cylinders or fewer, the lack of overlap in the [power strokes](/wiki/Power_stroke_(engine) "Power stroke (engine)") results in a pulsating delivery of torque to the [flywheel](/wiki/Flywheel "Flywheel"), causing a torsional vibration along the crankshaft axis.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Such vibration, if excessive, may be minimised using a [harmonic damper](/wiki/Harmonic_damper "Harmonic damper").

### Exhaust manifold

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Exhaust manifold")]

The typical firing order for a boxer-four engine is for the left bank of cylinders to ignite one after another, followed by the right bank of cylinders (or vice versa), with the [firing interval](/wiki/Firing_order#Even_and_uneven_firing_order "Firing order") evenly spaced at 180 degrees. Traditionally, the exhausts from the two cylinders on each bank were merged, with the resulting uneven exhaust pulses causing a characteristic "flat-four burble" exhaust sound.

The other common exhaust configuration (such as used by Subaru since the mid-2000s) is to pair the cylinders with a firing interval offset of 360 degrees, in order to optimise the [exhaust pulses](/wiki/Exhaust_manifold#Exhaust_scavenging "Exhaust manifold").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) This configuration requires long exhaust manifolds, in order to pair the cylinders on opposite banks, and results in a less distinctive exhaust sound.

## Use in automobiles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Use in automobiles")]

### 1900–1935

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1900–1935")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Wilson-Pilcher_flat-4_engine.jpg/250px-Wilson-Pilcher_flat-4_engine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilson-Pilcher_flat-4_engine.jpg)

1904 [Wilson-Pilcher](/wiki/Wilson-Pilcher "Wilson-Pilcher") water-cooled engine

In 1900, the first flat-four engine was produced by [Benz & Cie](/wiki/Karl_Benz#Benz_&_Cie._and_the_Benz_Patent_Motorwagen "Karl Benz"), based on Benz's 1897 "contra" flat-twin engine.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This engine was used in Benz racing cars, produced 20 hp (15 kW), had a displacement of 5.4 L (330 cu in) and was designed by Georg Diehl.

London company [Wilson-Pilcher](/wiki/Wilson-Pilcher "Wilson-Pilcher") released its first car in 1901, which was powered by a flat-four engine. This engine was mounted longitudinally in the chassis, water-cooled, produced 9 hp (7 kW) and had a displacement of 2.4 L (146 cu in). Unusually for its day, the bore and stroke were equal, with each being 95 mm (3.7 in).

In 1902 the Buffum automobile was equipped with opposed four cylinder engines that were rated at 16 horsepower. Herbert H. Buffum produced an American Automobile called the Buffum in Abington, Massachusetts from 1903 to 1907.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Having previously produced flat-twin engines, the 1926 [Tatra 30](/wiki/Tatra_30 "Tatra 30") was the Czech company's first model powered by a flat-four engine. Tatra produced various flat-four engined model through the 1920s and 1930s.

### 1936–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1936–1999")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Flat-four_engine "Special:EditPage/Flat-four engine") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(December 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Flat_Four_Jowett_Engine_from_a_Jowett_Jupiter.jpg/250px-Flat_Four_Jowett_Engine_from_a_Jowett_Jupiter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flat_Four_Jowett_Engine_from_a_Jowett_Jupiter.jpg)

1952 [Jowett Jupiter](/wiki/Jowett_Jupiter "Jowett Jupiter") water-cooled engine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/1955_Porsche_550_Spyder_engine.jpg/250px-1955_Porsche_550_Spyder_engine.jpg)](/wiki/File:1955_Porsche_550_Spyder_engine.jpg)

1955 [Porsche 550 Spyder](/wiki/Porsche_550_Spyder "Porsche 550 Spyder") air-cooled engine

The 1936 [Tatra T97](/wiki/Tatra_T97 "Tatra T97") pioneered the rear-engined, air-cooled flat-four, backbone chassis layout (later used by the [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle")), and at the same time, though unrelated, came the [Steyr 50](/wiki/Steyr_50 "Steyr 50") from Austria, sporting a front boxer 4 engine with rear wheel drive. Also in 1936, English company [Jowett](/wiki/Jowett_Cars "Jowett Cars") expanded its model range from flat-twin engines to also include flat-four engines. Production of Jowett flat-four engines continued until 1954, when the [Jowett Javelin](/wiki/Jowett_Javelin "Jowett Javelin") saloon and [Jowett Jupiter](/wiki/Jowett_Jupiter "Jowett Jupiter") sports models ended production.

The longest production flat-four engine is the [Volkswagen air-cooled engine](/wiki/Volkswagen_air-cooled_engine "Volkswagen air-cooled engine"), which was produced from 1938 until 2006 and was most famously used in the rear-engined 1938–2003 [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle") and 1950–1983 [Volkswagen Transporter](/wiki/Volkswagen_Transporter "Volkswagen Transporter").[[11]](#cite_note-11) This air-cooled engine was designed by Porsche and was also used in the 1948–1965 [Porsche 356](/wiki/Porsche_356 "Porsche 356"), 1953–1956 [Porsche 550](/wiki/Porsche_550 "Porsche 550"), 1965–1969 [Porsche 912](/wiki/Porsche_912 "Porsche 912") and 1969–1976 [Porsche 914](/wiki/Porsche_914 "Porsche 914"). In 1984, to comply with exhaust emissions regulations a water-cooled version called the [Volkswagen Wasserboxer engine](/wiki/Volkswagen_Wasserboxer_engine "Volkswagen Wasserboxer engine") was introduced in the [Volkswagen Transporter (T3)](/wiki/Volkswagen_Type_2_(T3) "Volkswagen Type 2 (T3)")

During the 1960s and 1970s, several manufacturers produced flat-four engines including the air-cooled [Citroën flat-four engine](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn_GS#Mechanics "Citroën GS"), the water-cooled [Alfa Romeo flat-four engine](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo_Flat-4_engine "Alfa Romeo Flat-4 engine"), the water-cooled [Lancia flat-four engine](/wiki/Lancia_Flat-4_engine "Lancia Flat-4 engine") and the water-cooled [Subaru EA engine](/wiki/Subaru_EA_engine "Subaru EA engine").

Two important engines designed during this period, but never saw the light of day in series production, were the Morris 800cc side valve engine by Alec Issigonis in 1947 originally destined for the Morris Minor, and the Ferguson 2.2 litre SOHC engine by Claude Hill in 1966 as part of the R5 vehicle research project.

### 2000–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000–present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Subaru_BRZ_engine_%2820413082204%29.jpg/250px-Subaru_BRZ_engine_%2820413082204%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Subaru_BRZ_engine_(20413082204).jpg)

2012–present [Subaru FA](/wiki/Subaru_FA_engine "Subaru FA engine") water-cooled engine

By 2000, most manufacturers had replaced flat-four engines with inline-four engines. A notable exception is Subaru, with the water-cooled [Subaru EJ engine](/wiki/Subaru_EJ_engine "Subaru EJ engine") being available in turbocharged form in the [Subaru WRX](/wiki/Subaru_WRX "Subaru WRX") sports sedan and its [World Rally Car](/wiki/World_Rally_Car "World Rally Car") counterpart. Subaru's adoption of [all-wheel drive](/wiki/All-wheel_drive "All-wheel drive") was a factor in retaining the flat-four engine, since the shorter length of this engine assists in fitting the all-wheel drive components into the chassis.[[12]](#cite_note-popsci-12) Although it is more expensive than an inline-four engine, the flat-four engine allows Subaru to build an all-wheel drive vehicle at little extra cost from two-wheel drive.[[12]](#cite_note-popsci-12)

In 2012, a naturally aspirated version of the [Subaru FA engine](/wiki/Subaru_FA_engine "Subaru FA engine") was used in the [Toyota 86](/wiki/Toyota_86 "Toyota 86") (also called the "Subaru BRZ" and "Scion FR-S") rear-wheel drive sports coupe.[[13]](#cite_note-Bonk-13) This engine is water-cooled, has [gasoline direct injection](/wiki/Gasoline_direct_injection "Gasoline direct injection"), produces 147 kW (197 hp) and has a displacement of 2.0 L (122 cu in).

The 2016 [Porsche Boxster/Cayman (982)](/wiki/Porsche_982 "Porsche 982") mid-engined sports cars downsized from a naturally aspirated flat-six engine to a turbocharged flat-four engine, Porsche's first flat-four since the mid-1970s. This engine is produced in displacements of 2.0–2.5 L (122–153 cu in) and produces up to 365 hp (272 kW).[[14]](#cite_note-14) Several reviewers criticised the Boxster/Cayman for an uninspiring engine sound.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 2025, Chinese automaker [BYD Auto](/wiki/BYD_Auto "BYD Auto") introduced its first flat-four engine in the [Yangwang U7](/wiki/Yangwang_U7 "Yangwang U7") luxury sedan, marking a modern revival of the boxer engine layout. This water-cooled, turbocharged powerplant combined direct injection and hybrid technology, delivering 268 hp (200 kW) while maintaining a compact design ideal for the sedan’s all-wheel-drive system. The engine drew inspiration from Porsche’s turbocharged flat-four units and showcased BYD’s push into high-performance powertrains alongside its electric vehicle lineup.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Use in motorcycles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Use in motorcycles")]

See also: [Motorcycle engine](/wiki/Motorcycle_engine "Motorcycle engine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Glengine.jpg/250px-Glengine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glengine.jpg)

[Honda GL1000](/wiki/Honda_Gold_Wing "Honda Gold Wing")

Most motorcycles with four-cylinder engines use a [straight-four engine](/wiki/Straight-four_engine "Straight-four engine") layout (most are flat plane, except Yamaha R1/R1M having crossplane), however, several flat-four engine engines have been used in [shaft drive](/wiki/Drive_shaft#Motorcycle_drive_shafts "Drive shaft") motorcycles:

* 1938–1939 [Zündapp K800](/wiki/Z%C3%BCndapp "Zündapp") (air-cooled)
* 1974–1987 [Honda Gold Wing](/wiki/Honda_Gold_Wing "Honda Gold Wing") (liquid-cooled)
* 1955–1956 [Wooler 500cc](/wiki/Wooler_(motorcycles)#Post_WW2_motorcycles "Wooler (motorcycles)") (air-cooled)
* 1981–1982 [BFG 1300](/w/index.php?title=BFG_1300&action=edit&redlink=1 "BFG 1300 (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/BFG_(entreprise) "fr:BFG (entreprise)")], using the air-cooled [Citroën flat-four](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn_GS#Mechanics "Citroën GS") car engine. Approximately 450 were built, with one quarter of these purchased by the French police.[[20]](#cite_note-CitroenetBFG-20)[[21]](#cite_note-CitroenetBoxer4-21)

## Use in aircraft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat-four_engine&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Use in aircraft")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/ULPower_UL260i.jpg/250px-ULPower_UL260i.jpg)](/wiki/File:ULPower_UL260i.jpg)

[ULPower UL260i](/wiki/ULPower_UL260i "ULPower UL260i") aircraft engine

[Light aircraft](/wiki/Light_aircraft "Light aircraft") commonly use flat-four engines with displacements up to 6.4 L (390 cu in) from manufacturers such as [Rotax](/wiki/Rotax "Rotax"), [Lycoming Engines](/wiki/Lycoming_Engines "Lycoming Engines"), [Continental Motors](/wiki/Teledyne_Continental_Motors "Teledyne Continental Motors") and [Franklin Engine Company](/wiki/Franklin_Engine_Company "Franklin Engine Company").

For [radio-controlled aircraft](/wiki/Radio-controlled_aircraft "Radio-controlled aircraft"), flat-four engines with displacements of 40–50 cc (2.4–3.1 cu in) are produced by companies such as [O.S. Engines](/wiki/O.S._Engines "O.S. Engines").

A notable recent flat-four aero-engine is the [side-valve](/wiki/Flathead_engine "Flathead engine") [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") [D-Motor LF26](/wiki/D-Motor_LF26 "D-Motor LF26").[[22]](#cite_note-22) Although the side-valve format has long been abandoned for most automotive applications because its combustion chamber is a bar to high engine [rpm](/wiki/Rpm "Rpm"), the massively over-square (1.295:1) D-Motor is a very simple, low-revving, compact, reliable lightweight aero-engine (without the heavy complication of [ohv](/wiki/Ohv "Ohv") valve-gear)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

If a valve in a side-valve engine should stick open, it cannot hit the piston and cause engine failure; the engine will continue with just that cylinder inoperative. This is an important safety feature for aero-engines.
